nodes.k: First Greek natural philosophy; sought the arche (basic stuff) of reality.
Reality as number and harmony; soul transmigration.
Being is one, unchanging; motion and plurality are illusion.
All is atoms and void; mechanistic materialism.
Rhetoric, relativism, 'man is the measure of all things'.
Eternal Forms; the sensible world as imperfect copy.
Substance, the four causes, virtue, empirical inquiry.
Reject convention; live in accord with nature.
Virtue is the only good; live according to reason/nature.
Pleasure as absence of pain (ataraxia); atomist physics.
Radical skepticism; suspend judgment for tranquility.
Emanation from 'the One'; mystical ascent of the soul.
Faith meets Aristotelian logic in the medieval university.
Aquinas' synthesis of Aristotle and Christian theology.
Universals are mere names; only particulars exist.
Greek philosophy in the Islamic Golden Age.
Reason and Torah; Aristotelianism meets scripture.
Return to classical sources; human dignity and letters.
Knowledge grounded in reason and innate ideas.
All knowledge derives from sense experience.
Reason, liberty, progress, critique of authority.
Reality structured by mind/spirit; system-building after Kant.
Feeling, nature, genius, and the will over cold reason.
The greatest happiness for the greatest number.
History as class struggle; critique of capital.
Knowledge from observable, scientific facts only.
Philosophy of life, will, and becoming.
Describe experience as it presents itself to consciousness.
Existence precedes essence; freedom, anxiety, meaning.
The theory and art of interpretation and understanding.
Vienna Circle: only verifiable statements are meaningful.
Dissolve confusions by attending to everyday use.
How theories are confirmed, falsified, and replaced.
Justice, liberty, rights via rigorous argument.
Critical theory of culture, domination, and reason.
Meaning arises from underlying systems of difference.
Destabilizes fixed structures, meaning, and the subject.
Truth and meaning judged by practical consequences.
The unconscious, drives, and early experience shape the mind.
Social and cultural revisions of Freud.
Mind studied by its adaptive function.
Only observable behavior is the proper object of study.
The whole is other than the sum of its parts.
How mind and behavior change across the lifespan.
How others shape thought, feeling, and behavior.
Freedom, meaning, mortality in lived experience.
Study of flourishing, strengths, and well-being.
Spiritual and peak dimensions of experience.
End of the Vedas; the nature of Brahman and self.
Non-dualism: Atman is Brahman; the world is appearance.
Disciplined practice to still the mind and free the self.
Logic and epistemology; valid means of knowledge.
Ritual, duty, and the authority of the Vedas.
Materialism and skepticism; only the perceived is real.
Non-violence, many-sidedness of truth, liberation of the soul.
The Buddha's path: suffering, its cause, and cessation.
'Way of the elders'; the Pali canon and the arhat ideal.
The 'great vehicle'; the bodhisattva and emptiness.
The 'middle way'; all phenomena are empty of essence.
Direct insight beyond words; meditation and sudden awakening.
Virtue, ritual, family, and the cultivated person.
Harmony with the Dao; wu wei (effortless action).
Order through strict law and state power.
Confucian revival absorbing metaphysics of li (principle).
Japanese philosophy fusing Zen and Western thought.
The way of the warrior; honor, discipline, loyalty.
Joseon debates on principle (li) and material force (qi).
Founder of Greek philosophy; water as first principle.
The boundless (apeiron) as origin of all.
Number and harmony; transmigration of souls.
Everything flows; unity of opposites; the Logos.
Defended Eleatic monism; being is infinite.
Four roots (earth, air, fire, water); Love and Strife.
'Man is the measure of all things.'
Theory of Forms; the Republic; founder of the Academy.
Logic, metaphysics, ethics, biology; the Lyceum.
Pupil of Socrates; founder of Cynicism.
The dog; lived in a jar, mocked convention.
Founder of skepticism; suspension of judgment.
Pleasure as tranquility; atoms; the Garden.
Second head of the Stoa; Hymn to Zeus.
Roman orator; transmitter of Greek philosophy.
Epicurean poet of 'On the Nature of Things'.
Stoic statesman; letters on living well.
Founder of Neoplatonism; the One, Intellect, Soul.
Consolation of Philosophy; bridged antiquity & Middle Ages.
Logician of universals; the ethics of intention.
Summa Theologica; synthesis of Aristotle & faith.
The 'subtle doctor'; haecceity, univocity of being.
'Philosopher of the Arabs'; introduced Greek thought.
Ibn Sina; metaphysics, the flying-man argument.
Ibn Rushd; great commentator on Aristotle.
Founder of sociology and philosophy of history.
Guide for the Perplexed; reason and Torah.
Inventor of the essay; skeptical self-study.
Father of empiricism and the scientific method.
'I think, therefore I am'; mind-body dualism.
Pascal's wager; reasons of the heart.
Occasionalism; we see all things in God.
Mind as blank slate; natural rights; tolerance.
Monads; best of all possible worlds; calculus.
The New Science; cycles of history.
Idealism: to be is to be perceived.
Skeptic of causation and the self; empiricism.
Separation of powers; 'Spirit of the Laws'.
Moral sentiments; the wealth of nations.
Critique of Pure Reason; the categorical imperative.
Language, culture, and the spirit of a people.
Vindication of the Rights of Woman.
The self positing the world; subjective idealism.
Dialectic; the unfolding of Spirit in history.
Founder of utilitarianism; the felicific calculus.
The world as Will and Representation; pessimism.
God as projection of human nature.
Father of existentialism; the leap of faith.
Will to power; eternal recurrence; 'God is dead'.
The human condition; banality of evil.
Ethics as first philosophy; the face of the Other.
Phenomenology of perception and the body.
Hermeneutics of texts and the self.
The absurd; the myth of Sisyphus.
Founder of modern logic; sense and reference.
Physicalism; the boat rebuilt at sea.
Tractatus; language games; the limits of language.
Logical structure of the world; semantics.
The 'ghost in the machine'; category mistakes.
Speech acts; how to do things with words.
Two dogmas of empiricism; the web of belief.
A Theory of Justice; the veil of ignorance.
Art in the age of mechanical reproduction.
Dialectic of Enlightenment; the culture industry.
Mythologies; the death of the author.
The mirror stage; the unconscious as language.
Colonialism and psychology of the oppressed.
Lacan meets Hegel and ideology critique.
Being and Event; the fidelity to truth.
The state of exception; bare life.
Field theory; founder of social psychology.
Zone of proximal development; social mind.
The magical number seven; cognitive revolution.
Traditional compiler of the Vedas and Mahabharata.
Founder of the Nyaya school of logic.
Freedom from the known; no authority.
The Buddha; the Four Noble Truths.
Sixth patriarch of Chan; sudden awakening.
Attributed founder of Daoism; the Dao De Jing.
'A white horse is not a horse'.
Human nature is bad; ritual reforms it.
Unity of knowledge and action; innate moral mind.
Founder of Soto Zen in Japan.
Swordsman; The Book of Five Rings.
Korean Neo-Confucian; rival view to Toegye.
Situationist who saw modern life as a 'society of the spectacle' where images replace lived experience.
Korean-German critic of the 'burnout society': in the name of freedom, we exploit ourselves.
Diagnosed 'capitalist realism' — the sense there is no alternative — and its mental-health toll.
Sociologist who saw the self as a performance staged for others, front-stage and back.
Anthropologist who coined 'bullshit jobs' and rethought debt, work and value.
The Cynic who lived in a jar and mocked convention — freedom through radical self-sufficiency.
Argued civilization is a vast defense against the terror of death.
Framed the 'hard problem' of consciousness and defended the reality of experience.
Asked what it is like to be a bat — subjectivity resists objective reduction.
Argues no one ever had a self — only a transparent 'ego tunnel'.
Argued personal identity 'is not what matters' — a Western echo of no-self.
Philosopher of the extended and predictive mind.
Defends analytic idealism: reality is fundamentally mental.
Neuroscientist: consciousness as a 'controlled hallucination' of predictive brains.
Cognitive scientist: perception is a fitness-tuned interface, not a window on truth.
Psychiatrist of the divided brain and its two ways of being.
Whose readiness-potential experiments challenged conscious free will.
Proposed the global-workspace theory of consciousness.
Maps the 'global neuronal workspace' — how information becomes conscious.
Built Integrated Information Theory: consciousness is integrated information.
Pursues the neural correlates of consciousness; champion of IIT.
Sees the self as a 'strange loop' — a pattern that refers to itself.
Co-founded enactivism; bridged cognitive science and Buddhist practice.
Founded cybernetics — control and communication by feedback.
Historian: humans cooperate at scale through shared fictions.
Critic of disembodied digital life and the 'extinction of experience'.
Sufi metaphysician of waḥdat al-wujūd, the unity of all being.
Adapted Buddhist mindfulness into clinical MBSR.
Developed Acceptance and Commitment Therapy and psychological flexibility.
Natural selection: design without a designer, reframing life and mind.
Dialogical philosopher of I–Thou: we become persons only in genuine relation.
Psychologist who recovered the 'different voice' of care in moral reasoning.
Built an ethics and a pedagogy founded on caring relation.
Moral philosopher and novelist of attention and 'unselfing'.
Philosopher-mystic of attention, affliction and grace.
Philosopher of capabilities, emotion and the fragility of goodness.
Economist-philosopher; development as the expansion of real freedoms.
Reopened virtue ethics and the philosophy of intention.
Communitarian critic of modern moralism; virtue needs tradition.
Jurist who reframed Buddhism as a path of social liberation.
The tertön who revealed the Bardo Thödol.
Process philosopher; reality as becoming, not static substance.
Christian mystic of the soul's union with the Godhead.
Scholastic who argued God's existence from the idea of God.
Theorist of free will, higher-order desires and caring.
Neuroscientist-philosopher; critic of free will and of religion.
Founder of the Rinzai line of Chan; teacher of shock and immediacy.
Legalist chancellor who built the Qin's centralized order.
Systematizer of yin–yang and the five phases.
